operating_state: RTX generated $22.5 billion in quarterly revenue, up 12% year-over-year, driven by organic growth across all segments from commercial aftermarket services expansion and defense contract execution. Nine-month revenue reached $64.4 billion with organic growth, while Raytheon secured significant defense bookings including Patriot systems and AMRAAM missiles contracts.
cost_structure: Cost of sales represented 79.6% of revenue in Q3, improving from 79.9% despite organic cost increases from higher sales volume. Nine-month gross margin improved to 20.3% from 18.9%, benefiting from absence of prior-year charges including Raytheon contract termination and Collins titanium sourcing costs. Company-funded R&D declined to 3.0% of sales from 3.7% in the prior year.
material_events: RTX completed the sale of Collins' actuation and flight control business for $1.2 billion proceeds, generating a gain. The company repaid $2.25 billion in debt during the nine-month period. A cybersecurity incident affected MUSE passenger processing software in September 2025, causing flight delays but no material financial impact expected, while investigation remains ongoing with potential regulatory inquiries.
macro_exposures: Universal tariffs imposed since February 2025 have prompted retaliatory measures, with higher tariff costs impacting commercial OEM profitability. The U.S. government shutdown beginning September 30, 2025 creates operational risks including potential stop work orders and payment delays. Geopolitical tensions continue with Chinese sanctions against Raytheon operations, ongoing Russia-Ukraine conflict supply chain effects, and foreign cash holdings creating currency considerations.
forward_outlook: The July 2025 reconciliation act provides supplementary DoD funding through 2029, including missile defense and munitions programs where RTX is well-positioned. RTX expects full-year 2025 cash impact related to the Powder Metal Matter including customer compensation and partner recovery timing. The company maintains revolving credit capacity expiring August 2028.
uncertainty_profile: Pratt & Whitney's powder metal matter requires accelerated GTF fleet inspections with elevated aircraft-on-ground levels affecting cash flow timing. The company faces potential prosecution risk if DOJ determines DPA violations during the monitoring period, with additional SEC enforcement exposure and Special Compliance Officer oversight through 2027. Supply chain disruptions persist from geopolitical conflicts, labor shortages, and material constraints affecting rare earth elements and microelectronics procurement.
